completeness.rationale: All seven expected extracts are present: the 1-in-3 intermittent pattern, the ~2-day timeline, the coincidence with a role change, analyst-role specificity, editor-role users being unaffected, multiple users affected, and the load-balanced environment (correctly hypothesized even though the reporter never confirmed it explicitly). The summary also captures the cross-browser nature and proposed test case beyond what was required.

accuracy.rationale: The triage hits the second acceptable diagnostic path (analyst-role specificity + timing) and goes further by correctly hypothesizing the load balancer with a stale per-instance deployment. The recommended fix explicitly says 'a rolling deploy or config sync issue may have left one or more instances without the analyst role definition,' which is essentially the canonical root cause. No material inaccuracies.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked three substantive rounds of questions and resolved with a strong basis. The one obvious gap: it never asked whether a system deployment or configuration push happened around the same time as the role assignment — that single question from the admin perspective could have confirmed the stale-deployment hypothesis directly. Everything else was well-covered.

economy.rationale: The turn-1 question about navigation method (bookmark vs. link vs. direct URL) turned out to be low-value — it didn't affect diagnosis. The browser/OS questions in turn 2 were generic but defensible given that cross-browser behaviour was worth ruling out quickly. The remaining questions were well-targeted. One question could have been dropped or combined.

question_quality.rationale: The standout move was in turn 3: the agent immediately recognised the significance of the off-hand mention of 'the new analyst role' and pivoted to a focused set of questions to confirm role-correlation and timing. The reporter had not connected those dots themselves and was attributing the problem to 'network issues.' The question about whether teammates seeing 403s are all on the analyst role was particularly diagnostic.

actionability.rationale: The summary includes clear reproduction steps, a well-reasoned root cause hypothesis pointing directly at load-balanced instances with stale RBAC, four numbered investigative steps, a concrete proposed test case, and an honest information-gaps list. A developer could open server logs and begin checking instance-level RBAC config immediately.

notable_strengths: Immediately recognised and followed up on the 'analyst role' detail the reporter mentioned in passing, pivoting the entire diagnostic direction in turn 3
Correctly hypothesised the load-balancer / stale-instance pattern from reporter-side evidence alone, without needing server access
Triage summary is unusually actionable: reproduction steps, numbered fixes, a test case, and explicit information gaps all in one place
notable_weaknesses: Never asked whether a recent deployment or configuration push coincided with the role assignments — one admin-facing question that could have confirmed the stale-deployment hypothesis
Turn-1 navigation-method question (bookmark vs. URL vs. in-app link) added no diagnostic value and could have been replaced with something more targeted
most_insightful_question: Could you help us confirm the correlation? Specifically: Were you recently switched to this new analyst role yourself? Among the teammates seeing the 403s, are they all on the new analyst role, or are any of them on a different role? Do you know roughly when the analyst role switch happened — does the timing line up with when the 403s started?
missed_opportunities: Ask whether any system-level deployment, configuration push, or maintenance window occurred around the same time as the role assignments — this would have surfaced the failed deployment directly

Ask whether the analyst role is newly created or a pre-existing role that users were migrated into — this distinction matters for whether the role definition itself is the issue vs. a deployment artefact
